text: We describe a case of robotic left colectomy with staple colorectal anastomosis using indocyanine green fluorescence angiography. The patient is a fifty-three years old female presented with abdominal pain and cedropanic anemia. Surgery was indicated after colonoscopy showing an obstructive adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon and a CT scan showing no sign of metastasis. The DaVinci XI robotic system is docked on the left side of the patient. The table is adjusted in the Tran-Delenburg position tilted on the right. Four robotic ports are placed on a diagonal line from the right femoral head to where the left midclavicular line crosses over the left subcostal mug. The robotic port four, the red one on the figure, is used for a twelve millimeter end of wrist. Each port is placed at a distance of eight centimeters from the others. A five millimeter air seal assistant port is placed lateral to the midclavicular line. Targeting is set on the left iliac vessel.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a middle to lateral approach after visualizing the lesion on the sigmoid colon. The left ureter is identified during dissection. The anterior mesenteric artery is dissected approximately 1.5 centimeters from the aorta and sectioned using a 45-millimeter EndoWrist instrument with vascular recharge. Monopolar scissors and bipolar fenestrated graspers are used for dissection. The inferior mesenteric vein is dissected, clipped, and sectioned.

The surgeon employs an extended medial-to-lateral approach for splenic flexure mobilization. The inferior mesenteric vein is ligated at the lower border of the pancreas, and the superior surface of the pancreas is separated from the retroperitoneum, opening the lesser sac. The gastrocodic and pancreatic mesocodic attachments along the tail of the pancreas are divided. A sterile gauze is placed near the splenic hilum before sectioning the gastrocolic ligament. The total fascia is dissected to achieve complete mobilization of the left colon.

The surgeon transects the rectum at least five centimeters from the colic cancer using a vessel sealer, preparing the rectal stump for transaction. The rectum is then transected with a 45-millimeter endobriste grain recharge, and the colon is extracted through a five-centimeter incision.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green fluorescence angiography to identify the proximal transection margin of the colon. The EEA stapler is inserted and secured to perform the colon-hepatic end-to-end anastomosis. The vascular viability of the intestinal tissue is rechecked using fluorescence angiography, confirming a good vascular supply.
